I was blessed that evening, with the gift of a ‘thornyapple’ seed pod full wonder and awe and delusion, hallucination and other wierd trippy stuff. I had to find the traditional relationship to the plant as I was already fascinated.

It seems that I’ve been given one of the local spiritual allies. I say local s in North America. Apparently Datura Inoxia has been in these parts for quite a while though thought to have originated in many places including the Middle East, Central America and China. I have read only one account of First Nation use as far north as Canada and I haven’t seen any coroborating story. I have read a lot about its use ceremonially within the tribes of Southern California and Mexico.

The most oft-told tale is of the initiation right using a tea made from Datura root. Young pubescent boys who are given this tea trip out for hours-to-days in a purification journey that gets them ready for manhood. They are said to leave their childhood behind. Other tribes use it for different adult rites as well. It is often stressed as a dangerous and poisonous plant that has killed people.

Here are a couple of the more interesting links about Datura and its traditional use:

http://www.escholarship.org/uc/item/37r1g44r

http://www.scribd.com/doc/19943424/Psychoactive-Plants-in-Tantric-Buddhism-Cannabis-and-Datura-Use-in-IndoTibetan-Esoteric-Buddhism

~*~
A recent letter to Garliq

Salut Ami!

I’m feeling so incredibly blessed with the gift of Datura you have bestowed. Thank you again for the thorny apple. When we got home, I put it up in the kitchen and after 3days it began to open revealing the cute little kidney beans inside. After a couple of more days, I peeled a little piece away at the other ‘slit’ as it hadn’t really done much since opening and I thought it may need my help since it was no longer on the bush. I also poured out some of the seeds onto the plate to study them. The pod still didn’t do much after a couple more days and so I decided to peel it open.

In all this time I have been doing MAD research on this beauty and have an encyclopedia of facts swimming around in my head. I’ll send along a couple of interesting links.

I decided upon peeling it open that I wouldn’t wash my hands, rather let the juices absorb into my fingers ~~and evaporate too~~ I was careful not to touch any mucous membrane until a while later. It didn’t take long for me to feel that something was unusual and I checked the bathroom mirror to find my well-dilated pupils. It wasn’t anything scary or deeply hallucinogenic but it was a little out of the norm, like when I would get into a staring-moment, I could feel the potential for transcendence seething.

There was a sinister edge to this precipice. It told me very clearly to tread carefully.

The feeling was with me well into the next day too. I can’t fathom what kind of presence of mind it would take to come back from slathering one’s body with crushed seed and fat. Can you Imagine tripping out for a week?

I find it curious that smoking of the leaf is a common athsma remedy. It makes me want to try it though I’m leary of growing the plant around a young Osha. We’ll see what the spring brings.
~*~

Every day, as the seeds are drying, I’m feeling more in awe with this entity that is sharing my kitchen. The pod has dried up and the seeds are ready to be stored. I’m still doing my research. I know that we will someday commune with intent. The set and setting have to be right though and until I feel completely comfortable they won’t.

I think I’ll probably have to put a few seeds into the ground before I smoke or eat or drink any of it.

posts.title: Twelfth Daime
posts.content:
Liberation!

An esteemed activist and Padrino visited Vancouver this past weekend and I was called to participate in the Works. It worked out that sOMa had a birthday party to attend in the city as well and so we came over as a Family for the weekend. The Works were held in a studio on the East Side; about a forty minute walk from where we were staying on ‘The Drive’. There were about fifty people in attendance with a few more men than women. The altar table was very small and there were eight seated around it. Padrino Nico welcomed us to the work. After opening prayers, We began into the Daime song. Except that we didn’t. I didn’t have the hymn in front of me and so was able to sing along rudimentaly. Nobody else other than those at the table were singing as Nico called us up one at a time to drink. The first drink was very big. Almost a full Dixie cup.

It was a very different Daime experience. The Ayahuasca was just like I remembered it but the ceremony itself was a radical Santo Daime experience for me. Of the room of fifty-or-so, there were never more than six of us singing. There wasn’t very much of a current to speak of. I’m happy that I sat close to the table so that I could feel what was there. The Force was strong. People hit the floor pretty quickly, before I was even feeling anything. There wasn’t the same connection as in any Work I’d ever been to. There is a very different experience when we’re all participating in the same actions. This lack of connection wasn’t a bad thing, so much as it was a challenge for me. I think that it left me feeling a little bit vulnerable. A typical kind of feeling when actuality doesn’t meet with expectation. This may also have been what triggered some of the feelings of self-defense I experienced (thanks to Mike for the suggestion that that my obsessiveness about crossing my arms, fingers, legs could have been the case) which led to me not allowing myself to let go fully, to surrender into the experience.

We began by singing the Oracao, which was a Joy to behold!. It felt so wonderful to experience that particular Hinario again as it was the most familiar from my Daime days in Toronto. After that we went into concentration. There was lots of intensity. A lot of people were lying down and there were no guardians on the men’s side except for the Padrino himself. The women had a couple Fardado taking care of them. Like I said, a very strange Daime work. For me, the concentration was when the Daime became intense:

I came to realize after that the hymns kept me well distracted from what she was trying to accomplish with me, and when I settled into a meditative space, It went to work. And I went to work running from it. The intense energy was building inside of me and it was getting real swirly and I felt the need to puke come on. I felt very afraid of that for a lot of reasons, mostly insecurity, and I suppressed the sensation and bent myself forward into my knees. The intensity increased INTENSELY!!!!: Just like a hot rocket blasting up and through me it took off and exploded out of me in the most incredible sweat!

Then the Daime was done. Such a strange and sudden shift. It was like: OK, time for another drink and it felt like much too long before we drank again. When we did, it was a different Daime. I don’t know if it was just a different batch or if it was made with a different variety of Caapi, or what! but it was horrid. I’ve drunk it before at a couple of different Works. It didn’t bring me back into the space I was in before yet allowed a coasting of the Buzz. Which wasn’t unpleasant at all but kinda confused. I don’t think I’m the only one who felt that.

Then Nico decided that he needed to up the energy of the Work and so we moved all the chairs and set up for a dance. It was a crazy scene! Half a hundred people tripping out on Ayahuasca trying to organize themselves into straight lines and then perform the two-step in sync. It was hilarity and a really fun way to bond within the group (also a great way to get the room cleaned up!). After we closed the Work, we all gathered around the Salao and had a sharing Pow Wow where some folks shared some experience. I shared some blueberries with Daniel.

I felt a sadness while walking home. There was part of me that felt that the church had been made a mockery of. I went through a lot of analysis about my experience with puking and realized that the suppression likely curtailed a transcendental experience much like my first Daime trip. That time, I had decided to puke. This time I decided not to and was rewarded with a sweat and then a longing. I made a decision to puke on day two, if the need came up. I couldn’t believe I was about to do all that again. Especially when I felt so exhausted after the long walk up to home. Blueberries were not enough. I decided that I would eat Sunday morning before the work.

posts.title: Thirteenth Daime
posts.content:
Somebody knew what was going on in there....

When I arrived on Sunday, I was shown a truly incredible photo of a rainbow coming out from the top of the studio, evening sun-lit mountains beyond the harbor. The picture, snapped the previous day at around four (likely when we were emerging from the first serving), was beautiful testament to the goings-on within the studio.

It felt like there was a more of a comeraderie this time. There was also a confidence in the group. For several of the group, yesterday had been their first experience with Ayahuasca. For others, their first time around so many other people. Today, we were all a little more clean than yesterday. Open vessels ready to chart a course to…

Liberation…
Union…

This time I was able to sing the opening Daime hymn fully along and I noticed that others were singing as well. The first serving was almost as big as the previous day’s. And it tasted almost like both of yesterday’s combined. I felt it turn on the moment it hit my mouth.

We sang the Oracao again and the Daime Force came on strong. During the concentration, I had to find a place to sit with a bucket, as feelings of nausea washed over me. It never got to puking, however. I spent a good amount of time wrapped in my green afghan banket, on my knees with my forehead on the floor. There was a bliss in this position that allowed me to not have to worry about vomiting and also to have good navigation through psychic terrain. I never really hit an intensity like the previous day but coasted on an energy that felt like it was almost there. It felt like I was down for a long time. The room was a very chaotic place with a lot of people in refuge on the floor. A lot of Yoga and other movement.

With the second serving, Padrino Nico let us know that we could ask for more if we felt called. After drinking, I felt called right away. I let it sit for a bit and then followed a flow of others who wanted a little bit more. So that my third serving was about as big as the second. And I wondered if I hadn’t done it this time…

I can’t quite remember what we sang. It’s very vague. Through the work, the undercurrent of the beautifully precise guitar work helped to keep a grounding. There was a time where the hymns were ones that I didn’t recognize. I spent a good amount of time on the floor again. This time I was able to sit with my back against the wall. Again, I didn’t need the bucket but it felt good to have it around. It also felt so good to not have a guardian imploring that I return to the work. I hit an intense peak of pulsing energy which settled into a harmonious humm all around. During the concentration, I noticed a lot of movement. There seemed to be a lot of people communicating with entities.

After a lengthy concentration, the Padrino called us all to dance again. It was a similar scene to Saturday but while it was happening, something potent was happening inside of me, like a magnetic field was establishing itself. I moved myself to a safe place and hooked myself into the field. It’s motion was somewhat in sync with the back and forth of the room. Which rocked me around a pivoting point. I’m sure the danced would have looked something like a whirling dervish. While I was in this, It was the most rapturous sensation! My hands were in prayer position at my lips and nose and tears were streaming down my face. I wasn’t crying so much as expelling tears. There were no sobs, almost as if my eyes were irritated, but they weren’t, just teary. It was a blissful release. Full of light and its subsiding was so gradual and peaceful and almost like it still hasn’t fully gone away a week later.

The Work closed and I knelt down near a bucket again and then after a few minutes, took off my glasses and lay down on my back, Green Blankie (Thank you Green Blankie!) around me. My breaths were so full and long and it seemed like eternities between them. The room was very quiet, for a room full of people who had just finished a work. Often my exhales would be accompanied by a deep sigh. We were like that for a while and then began the sharing portion. I was in an ethereal space, not yet able to sit and everyone around me felt so raw and open. Those who shared told of very emotional and revelatory experiences. There were a lot of gifts and we were all so grateful a group to have been together for that experience.

I was still tripping as I got changed and left. Walking out into Vancouver felt like a courageous act, leaving the mystic realm that we created. Taking that bit with me into the night. Finding my way home. The force remained strong well into the night.

I feel so free in terms of my relationship with the Daime now. I also feel like I have witnessed something primal. THIS is how communing with the sacred plants is meant to be. We all held a sacred space for ourselves and for each other. There was an anchor of a Daime work going on that those who needed could hold onto. For others, They were in a place where they had the solitude they needed for the work to be done. There was no pressure for any of us to be any way other than how we needed to be. I know by this that the Daime is not an essential component to the Ayahuasca experience… there are many Yogic Paths.

This said, I would commune with the Daime in an authentic work again at the drop of a hat too, or even have one of my own!

I’m feeling almost ready to decoct my own brew. I may have even figured out the local plants!

posts.title: The Benson Grind
posts.content:
I didn’t have a clue it would be that steep. I knew that I was in for a good hike but I wasn’t expecting a spiritual experience!

When I was told that the route from Witchcraft Lake was steeper than the path from Westwood Lake, It still didn’t register that carrying my toddler up the mountain was going to be anything more than a moderate hike. Even Andrea was reassuring as we broke into the hike that it would be no problem. I think that Benson has such a calm presence to me that I couldn’t imagine it to be so steep. It’s the dominant land form in town, stretching to a kilometre above sea level right before us. It is dominant, yes but it’s not imposing. There are visible crags all over the East face that we see from Nanaimo but there is no rocky peak, so to speak. At 1023m, It’s not even yet out of the tree-line.

The two things I like most about Mt. Benson are that it looks a little like Mt. Sproat in Whistler, (which was my favorite visible land form when I lived there,) and that it’s covered with trees. I Love living in a city with this tree-furred snuggly beast watching over us. From the first time I heard that you could hike to the summit, I’ve had a notion that a psilocybin ceremony will happen up there for me someday.

It was a big group that began from Witchcraft lake which I’ve been told is at about 300m. The hike was put on through Moksha Yoga Nanaimo and as it was Laura’s event, my job was to be on childcare duty. I had a talk with sOMa a few days prior about how if he found it too challenging that I would happily hang back with him. I gave up on any expectation of making it to the top, though when asked as we departed, I stated that “I intend to make it to the top.”; again, without any expectation. It was a pretty liberating space in which to begin such a grueling journey.

It got steep pretty soon in and it didn’t take long for the burn to set into my thighs. I was deliberately staying back so as to allow little distraction for OshA to fall asleep. It kinda worked and he drifted in and out with the rhythm of my working up the mountain. It was so peaceful to be by ourselfs, the woods were dusty and the path made its way up using roots and fallen logs and rocky bluffs. And steep, ascending all the way. I had my first break, after a huge steep spot, at a place where someone had scratched 495m into the rock in a clearing. OshA awoke and I was so happy to be half way up the mountain! Laura caught up (yeah, I passed a bunch of them on the steep part;o) and decided to take OshA but soon decided that she preferred to stick with the group and so handed him back.

Of course, we weren’t really half way through the hike. That came a little farther up as we hit “the road” and were now in the Mt. Benson Regional Park. It was around this stretch that OshA decided he didn’t want to be carried anymore and since it was a pretty moderate part of things, I welcomed the break from his weight. It turned out that he could go about as fast as my tired dogs could take us anyway so it was a win-win. It was while walking in this stretch that I communed with Cannabis. An offering to the journey so far and what lay ahead. It’s strange how as I hit the road, the halfway point, I grew even less attached to the result yet even more certain of reaching the summit. I could hear voices up ahead for a while and it felt like a wonderful communal effort.

Most of all, the exhausted cardio workout I was getting a little break from was washing me over with its adrenalizing benefits (or whatever it is that’s going on!!). While I was enjoying the break, I was also excited about the next stage of the workout. As it got steep again, I strapped on OshA and we carried on up. My breath began pumping again and every step was a chugg-chugg of this machine I embodied right then. We were so sweaty and hot. It was so cleansing on that level, I’m sure. That can likely be the reason that it felt not at all unlike a Moksha Yoga class in how my being responds to that environment. I was feeling in awe at how hard I was able to work and at the same time, how absolutely marvelous it felt to be this completely worn out!

It felt like I was understanding what people mean with the “Runner’s High” I’ve heard about. Never having been a runner, I get it as the feeling of a Moksha class. It’s this place that leaves you in a place that’s open and receiving of the Message of the Universe. The Massage, if you will. It is a true and real religious experience. Experiential connection with the divine.

Despite my best efforts, I couldn’t catch up with the group. They always remained just a little bit ahead, within earshot. I heard sOMa as he was talking with Bill and Judy and Laura the whole way up. I’m amazed and so proud at how easy-going he found the trip up.

After an incredibly steep part, which I was told by a couple of hikers passing me was the steepest part on the journey, I rounded a bend, over a boulder and suddenly the voices were right there. “I’m at the top!!” I thought and was deflated, but only for a moment as I was told that the top was right up there. Where Laura pointed looked so far away but after a brief rest, there was no way I wasn’t making it the rest of the way. sOMa found his way with the group while Laura and I almost got lost… but found our way. OshA fell asleep again as I hit the end of the climb by myself, grunting :”AAAARGHHHH!” pulling myself and his sleeping bundle of Love up the steep slope using the thin trees and roots. It was such hard work and it was also somewhat transcendental; and about ten minutes later I was there. There were still a few people just making it up and there was jubilation abound.

I was distracted while looking for a spot for the babe to sleep but he awoke as I lay him down. I was elated and exhausted. Vibrating at a frequency that was… on the edge is the best way to say. It didn’t feel like flight was out of the question. I was in awe of the view. Seeing Mt. Arrowsmith and how it fit into its range was quite something. It was marvelous to behold a good chunk of the Salish Sea and to see the Coast Mountains. The city was so small and the downtown area where we live seemed a little farther away than the summit of Benson looks from there… An interesting note, there are three tall buildings in Nanaimo’s Downtown. At the time of our climbing, there was what seemed to be a cruise ship in the harbour (we later found out it was a freighter): The thing was AS BIG as the condo building… Crazy!

Then the crowd left and we were left up there, just the Loves and I didn’t want to leave. It was a blessing that Laura chose to take OshA down. We took the longer less steep route to the 1/2 way mark and then down through the woods where we met back up with a few of our crew. Such a feeling of bliss enveloped me as I emerged from the trailhead at the end.

At the time of this writing, we’re at four days past and the elation is about done. It still feels great to have done it! For the first two days after, all I wanted to do, all I thought about was going back up the mountain. My body felt so good toning up! Definitely a recommended journey. I can’t wait to try it without the extra weight.

posts.title: Church of the Universe
posts.content:
View FREE FOOD HERE in a larger map

The Assembly of the Church of the Universe is a religion I heard about in my early twenties. It is one of many entheogenic religions out there that use Cannabis as a sacrament. Naturally, I can get behind such a notion as Cannabis is used sacramentally in my spiritual practice as well. I don’t follow the Church much since having heard of them long ago. I just reconnected with the Idea when I thought of writing about my communion with plants, which has felt a recent deepening while I’ve been tending a Basil patch and the three remaining (of six) Garlic flowers.

And also, specific to this story, visiting a local Black Cap Raspberry (Rubus leucodermis) patch where I pick.

I’ve had many Churches over my life. When I say Church, I mean to say “Place where spiritual/religious practice takes place”. Important ones that come to mind for me are the water-filling spring North of Pickering, where people came from all over the region to fill their bottles and commune in their own ways. We regularly made the 50km return trip for our drinking water supply. Kind of like we do now, hitting Church at the tap behind the Duncan Garage. Best water I’ve ever tasted comes out of that tap. If there’s a band playing at the Garage Showroom then it’s an added bonus. Piper’s Lagoon is another spiritual place for me, climbing on windy bluffs, communing with rock and water, like I commune with rocks and water when I skip them into The Ocean, which can’t help but to ALLways be Church…

…And when I’m climbing around in a clearcut on a scorching July day, fully pantsed and sleeved, for the abundance of berries the Black Caps give forth. It’s a pretty special service out there: Upon entering the Salao, I ready my gear and walk in through the first path from the road. It is here that I will normally commune with the Sacrament. There is an instant connection of intent with the field around and I can almost audibly hear a brouhaha of berry goodness ready to jump into my bucket. Today, I was inspired to get some “HUck-a-berries” for OshA and so I opted to follow their trail, which inevitably led me to a marvelous little slice of Black Cap Heaven.

The thing I Love most about Black Caps is their palatability throughout a broad range of ripeness. From the earliest flush of juiciness to almost crispy on the plant, they exibit various degrees of sweetness that is mostly tastly and very versatile in its application. I also really Love how challenging they are to collect in my current patch.

I don’t know how old is the clearcut (pictured above) but it’s a mass of slash(?) that’s got a lot of Fireweed growing and trailing blackberries all over. It’s hard trecking where often I’m stumbling in between logs or tripping on prickly and always I’m feeling so grateful to have on long sleeves and pants. During the day it’s hot going, in the evening, the mosquitoes are ravenous.

As the day wore on today, my meditation with the berries yielded an abundance of fruit. Even though I was well hydrated, I felt so hot and heavy and in such a peaceful serene and egofree state. The buzzing of the bees ravaging the Fireweed was a continuous drone of hard work and it motivated me to fill the bucket. There was a family of Bald Eagles on hand too, soaring high up from the trees on top of the hill. Watching over me. I visited parts of the patch I hadn’t yet hit this year and went back to the spots I’d picked last week and in all ended up with more than five pounds of beautiful berries which will become some of the best candy going…

A spiritual experience in itself.

**

Church exists in whatever space we choose as sacred. For me, often that sacred space involves plants, if not in the locale then definitely in the Sacrament. The Church of the Universe is all around us whenever we need to use it it’s there. Sometimes we can find it in really great physical locales but it can be created out of anywheres… doesn’t even need a wheres!

Blessed Be.

posts.title: Moonset Over Lake Huron/Michigan
posts.content:
The sky out there, star-sparkled forever
Over the endless horizon, is busy
With wind of such Ferocious velocity,
Along with streaks of burning debris

And the pulsing flashes of the
Brilliant and continuous heat lightning;

While the Moon,
This ‘harvest’ Moon-set, huge and waxing half,
Glides quietly, all eerie and orange-like, past
It all and into the flat and brooding abyss

Of this churning inland sea
In front of me…

For a refreshing midsummer dip.
Or quite simply it’s the trip
Into tomorrow.

posts.title: Learning Empathy From Our Children
posts.content:
The theory is that Empathy is an innate state in humans.  When we look at what we’re doing to ourselves and each other(and everything else in our collective path,) it seems that this couldn’t be farther from the truth.  I had the opportunity to learn it experientially the other night while hanging with Henry and OshA in Finnegan’s room.  I was getting into the music making machine when OshA said “Poppa, Owee!”.  I looked up to see that 10 month-old Henry had landed somehow flat on his back.  OshA was telling me that he’d seen Henry fall over.

Henry was fine on his back and then OshA was struck with the notion to get down too.  I laughed and then followed suit.  What a beautiful gesture.  We hung out on our backs for what must have been three minutes but for what felt like forever, or at least like it could have gone on so long.

From watching OshA interact with such intent-full mimicry toward sOMa and to a lot of what else I see him encounter, is certainly evidence of an empathic way of being.  His identifying a ‘pain’ or ‘discomfort’ moniker to Henry’s situation of having fallen over is clearly another.

My job as his parent is to help him foster this way of being as he becomes himself more and more in this crazy mixed up world.  Given my experience with my first child, I don’t quite know if I know how to pull it off.  It feels like I’ve thwarted this in sOMa over years of unleashing my untamed programming upon him.  In this respect, I haven’t really been doing my job.

I have to trust that as I become more aware of how I tick, I will also become more able to have the relationship with sOMa that isn’t based on a power/control dynamic that I’ve helped foster.  As I watch what can be with OshA, I am learning some of what will help us grow.

Parenthood is a messed up and beautiful opportunity for personal and global development…  It’s a place where even the best-laid plans fall victim to circumstance and ill-placed reactionary decisions.  I feel so blessed to have the opportunity to share in the growing.

posts.title: Creating Compassionate Community, or Touching the Heart of the Centre of the Universe…
posts.content:
If there was one message for me that played itself over and over throughout the week it was speaking to the importance, even primacy, of the Need for Community.  A message that comes up for me again and again, and again this time.  This time I got to live it closely and intensely with my Family and fifty folk in a beautiful and remote setting on Salt Spring Island.  I was one of their cooks.

Kyle first pitched the Idea of the camp to me last April; A place where we would create community while engaging in NVC workshops and Empathy groups, to me, sounded like an incredible opportunity for connection.  We’re mighty fond of Ilan’s family and the folks we’ve met in Vancouver through them and so the Idea of spending a week camping and communing with that clan was well received.  I was so excited to get the invite in June.

_

When we arrived, late and harried on Tuesday evening, we were so warmly greeted by staff who had already arrived.  It was a blessing to have the support of everyone from the get-go.  Ingrid helped me get the wheel barrows back up to the parking lot, Jeannie was there to help prop the poles of the tent, there was food prepared and an immediate sense of belonging.  There was also a lot of letting go of agendas as the day unfolded in not at all the kind of way that the organizers had planned.

The next morning after one of the worst sleeps ever (on a slope and cold, fretfull somewhat slumber), I was awakened by the quietly-Joyful re-union of a couple of close friends right outside our tent.  We awoke to a breakfast ready at the camp and a busy first day of getting ourselves oriented as staff for this camp that has never been before.  Oh ya, did I mention that Tasha, the event co-ordinator was sick and maybe not even going to show.  There was also the issue of the walk between the camp and the hall where the workshops were being held, was much longer than the 20mins that we were told it was.

It was a good thing that Laura was there to lead us through a little Yoga practice in the sun on the rocks at the Ocean before our morning meeting.  Ilan and sOMa plotted their adventures in the rocks and with the driftwood as we met and then I found myself with some free time before lunch so I hung out with them and explored the beach.

Ruckle Provincial Park is a pretty neat place.  I found a Geo-Cache there once a few years ago.  Its campsite is really great and a fun place to be with children.  The Beaver Point Hall where the workshops were held is an old hall with no washrooms.  It’s got a great kitchen and wood all over the place.  A really super spot for the work that we were doing.

As people arrived through the Wednesday, I got to meet some new folks and connect with some old friends.  It was a jovial coming together.  sOMa’s fears of being the oldest kid there didn’t come true.  There were three kids older than him there.   The community grew to its apex and Thursday, the workshops began.

As a kitchen staff member, I had to report to duty half way through the workshops with Ingrid.  It hadn’t quite dawned on me signing up that it was going to be the case and it felt quite jarring and shocking in many ways during the camp.  The positive flipside is that the micro community of the kitchen, which consisted of Eliza as coordinator with Eden and myself as volunteers, was a really great place to be and create.  It was a free and open kitchen space where we each decided what tasks we wanted to do that would contribute to Joy.  I realized, importantly, that I have never worked in a kitchen where the food I was making resonated with my notions of what makes up a healthy diet~namely whole, minimally processed and fresh foods.  Preparing such nourishing goodness for four days has inspired me to seek out something like that as a way to earn a living.  There was a seriousness in the kitchen but also revelry!  Eden taught us a round and we got along stomping and singing while we created delectable dishes for our community…

The first day was especially hard for me.  Adjusting to the shock of missing half the lectures plus the full schedule left little time for family connection.  I found myself going to bed really grumpy, exhausted, disappointed.  Feeling regret about my decision to go as staff to ‘save money’ and thus, not trust abundance.

Travis and Leslee blessed us with the morning wake up song each day.  Each day a different song and each day a great way to awaken.  A very different wake up for our family:  Get up, pee, put on clothes, outside clothes, gather things from the tent for the day, rush up to the car, drive 2km to the hall,  eat breakfast……OOF!  It certainly kick-started the day.  And the pace didn’t really slow for us either.

In the morning workshop on the second day, Ingrid bid us into an exercise:  The group was divided in two:  Trees and Water.  The trees stood in their places and waited for a water to choose them.  Once the Water had made the choice and determined the parameters of the exchange (‘How close do you want to stand to this person’) they then began to talk (or not) for three or four minutes while the Tree listened in absolute silence.  I started out as water and went right next to me where Kyle was waiting.   I unloaded all that I was feeling about the previous day and my feelings around it while he listened.  At the end of time, we silently moved on… I chose another tree and had another few minutes to speak.  This time to someone unfamiliar to me.  Again, a release, a flood as I spoke what was percolating for me in the moment, including a growing fascination with the process.  The third Tree I visited was Laura and it felt so relieving to tell her everything that was going on while she silently took it all in.  My fourth tree was another stranger and I was amazed at the lightness in my being as it all came out again and again and I was simply being heard.  It was an incredible opening to Joy.

I then got to be Tree for four sessions.  One of the waters that visited  me did not really have anything to say and I knew none who came by.  It was really difficult to remain in complete listening silence.  I caught myself with an audible ‘Mmmmmm’ or ‘Hmmmm’ a couple of times.  It was work to not reach out and offer whatever to them: advice, stories, verbal understanding, I could offer Empathy with my eyes and body language but wow did I ever want to throw words at each of them.  It was a really important learning on the value of shutting up; for I can’t imagine having gotten to where I did for the speaking part of the exercise if people had chimed in with their two cents.  I missed the follow-up to the exercise due to camp commitments.  But I did get to cook.

A really great and important part of NVC work is Empathy.   It is through Empathy that I really understood the power of NVC.  If I could categorize what part of the week brought me the most value, it would be through the practice of group Empathy.  The adults were split into Empathy groups consisting of six people, one of whom, someone more experienced with the concepts, would be lead~essentially facilitator of the group.  Our group was called Salal and ‘Athene the Empathy Master’ was our leader.  The Empathy sessions were 50mins long.  After checking in, we decided who was most in need of empathy and that person was asked to share a situation.  The rest of us then made attempts to connect with their needs by guessing what kinds of feelings were associated with their story.  We were guided through the process by Athene who would stop us at times and talk through what kinds of things were happening with the subject, or the value of certain types of queries, specifics about how to word feelings.  It was like another course altogether and so incredibly valuable.

I received Empathy twice from the group, both times to really positive results.  The first time I got into some of my life story, basically the reasons for being at the camp, and felt some wonderful body sensations when connections happened, when people’s guesses landed.  I was left with a real sense of hope and a full feeling of connection.  The second time I asked to receive Empathy from a place of celebration as I was feeling really in a great space about being at the camp, and it was transcendental!  It was Athene who took me there.  We were outside on the edge of the woods sitting on stumps.  I don’t know what she called the process, maybe ‘Deepening the need’ or ‘uncovering the need’ but It was astonishing to me how simply we got to that space.

I told her that my need for community was being met by the camp.  She sat with that and then asked which need was met by that.  I thought about it and realized that it meant that my need for connection was being met.  Digging deeper, I found that my need for feeling valued was at the heart of that, which was meeting my need for self-connection and then it was an AHA moment:  We were there in the woods and I understood experientially, the connection of myself with the interconnected Oneness of it all.  I felt awash with such a Bliss.  An amazement at how alive the feeling was inside of me and at how easy it was to get to it.   I floated in this bliss for the rest of the session while we gave more Empathy and then we felt some mourning as it was our last full session together.

The thing that is really moving to me about this experience is that I’ve gotten myself back into the space since then.  I slip into it while I’m relating the story and even while thinking of it.  Whenever I retrace my steps I find myself there.  I’m beginning to wonder about whether it’s an innate state that I’ve uncovered.

I’m discovering the potent and glorious beauty of Empathy and at the same time realizing that it’s there all along… And it’s all we really want to do deep down.  We have but only to allow ourselves to access it through the years of Dominator Society  (or ‘Jackal’ as seen through NVC) conditioning.  Therin lies the really hard work of it all.  But it’s the ‘Good Work’, if you will.  The Work that will transform our Culture.

Another example of it being there was when Ingrid led the group into the main hall for a ‘Body NVC’ exercise.  I was excited that they were coming out so that we could see them while we were preparing the food.  So excited that in my distraction, I cut myself pretty severely.  The cut itself turned out to be not so serious but the force of it would have cut off my finger had it not been for my nail.  Perhaps it was this that sent me into shock.

After dressing the wound, I got back to work to finish the dish I was preparing.  They had decided to shut the barrier between the kitchen and the hall and they also requested that we work in silence to minimize distractions.  I finished up all I needed to get the dish going and then took repose.  I went outside and regrouped, had a pee and relaxed.  I came in and sat down for a bit.  Eliza brought me some Rescue Remedy.  Feeling pretty emotional, I got up to stir things and check when several people in the workshop had intense passionate releases one after the other…

I was overcome with the overwhelming mourning being expressed, burst into tears and had to sit down again.  It was astonishing how much I was affected by the emotional release.  I was in shock and amazed with being in shock at once.

The last day of the camp was busy again.  Everybody pitching in to clean up the two locations we occupied.  There was a joyous current to the business.  Partly there was a relief that we were through with the gruelling exercise of being at camp, partly it was celebration of what we’d accomplished and what we were taking with us out into the world.

The closing ritual was sublime and so deeply connecting for me.  As it progressed I became so connected with both the mourning of dissipation of our community and the celebration of what we created together.  I cried in full deep sobs for the final portion of the ritual.  And when we turned to walk out of the circle and into the world, I held on as long as I could.  The tears, such a comfort.  I felt so completed by community and so solitary at the same time.

There is already talk of a next year.  It seems I wasn’t the only one deeply affected by what we created.  I feel excited by all of it, especially by seeing how an Idea can manifest; and importantly by how this new language is making its way into my being, re-awakening my Empathic centres:  The Language of Life.

posts.title: Would That I Could
posts.content:
Would that I could enjoy with you, my Love:

A special selection of midsummer moments
In an oft-winded, flower-stained, dew-filled, meadow,
Presence, shared with strong, truthful thoughts, comments;
Sweet secretive glances leaving us goofy and mellow.

Serene, breathtaking engulfment of a warm sun-baked breeze
That swirls around our energy of ideas, dreams, notions.
Up above and through the passion of wild, swaying trees,
And farther away, even, than the most remote of all Oceans.

The melodic, playful rush of a fast brook that can’t be shushed
(never a  stress)
On the shore, a swift token gift inspired by this liquid excitement:
All the fury of fire in the sudden spark of a silent caress…
All the kind laughter of knowledge in a quick giggle of enlightenment.

posts.title: Urban Wildcrafting
posts.content:
In an era where issues of food security are becoming more prominent in most people’s lives, It feels like now, more than ever, knowing what food is available outside of traditional sources (like where you’re able to exchange money for groceries) can be a factor in comfort, health and even survival. Last night, at the request of Laura who was experiencing some swollen glands, sOMa and I went across the street to the field to get some chickweed that she wanted to use as a poultice. We were also having friends over for dinner and so I thought to gather a little extra that we could add to a salad. We ended up with a fair haul and, sensing sOMa’s enthusiasm for our spontaneous Wildcrafting adventure, I suggested we go and get some Hairy BitterCress from a patch I discovered the other day.

Hairy BitterCress

While over at the BitterCress patch, sOMa noticed some Wild Lettuce. Extremely bitter, wild lettuce is an excellent addition to a wild salad. We grabbed a little.

Wild Lettuce

I was enthralled at being taught of a plant from sOMa. It took me back to our days of foraging in Markham when he was really young. It affirmed for me the solidity of that base, even though our urban environment is very different, he still has the thirst for knowledge and communion with nature. A feeling of security ~food and otherwise~ was coming over me. Such an important part of our lives, food and too rarely are we connected with the conditions that bring it about. Gardening is one way. Wildcrafting is another.

At this point it was clear that we were in fact building a salad and while we thought we’d add some Kale from the Garden, I also thought about the FalseMint in the back yard.

False Mint

While we were gathering that, sOMa spotted a little bit of Dandelion and so we put a little more bitter into our mix.

Dandelion

The Kale never made it in.

We had heaven!! While Leah was bringing along a salad for dinner, we had an excellent and Uber-Nutritious amendment to the meal.

Wild Salad

Deep green and so full of flavor. If you’ve never eaten Chickweed, I suggest finding yourself a patch. It’s an amazing green and so full of Iron and other goodness. And it wasn’t hard to find. We have variety that compares to and even exceeds that of the shelves of a foodstore and it’s all right outside your door. FOR FREE!

The economic system that we’re in is failing, failing fast. The more you know how to get the food you need from around you, the less scary it will be when you can no longer get it from where you always have. Even Urban Nature is abundant. Plants aren’t going to let a little city get in the way of their thriving. They’re there for us. All we have to do is know what we’re looking for. Enjoy the buffet!

posts.title: The Mystical Magic of Co-Sleeping
posts.content:
There are few things I cherish more in this life than our Family Bed. Through the years of growing this family, the bed has gone through many different incarnations (even times of us not all together) all leading to the current setup of the four of us in our king-sized slumber-sled.

From the get-go, for both laura and I, co-sleeping was conscious act on our part to help put an end to generations of destructive and disconnecting patterning in our society (you know, the kind that leads to war and other addictions and abuses). We had both been influenced by an incredible book called Hello My Name is Chellis and I’m in Recovery From Western Civilization by Chellis Glendinning, and also Jean Liedloff’s The Continuum Concept and the books by Dr. Sears advocating attachment parenting. It felt like anything other than sleeping with our children would be offering them, and the world a grave disservice.

I can honestly say (and extremely mind-blowingly pleasantly so) that I didn’t have any Idea what I was in for in the months and then years to come and I don’t know if I’m ever gonna get over it when the boys want to leave.

For starters, on a study level, as in Life study: gathering knowledge and theories about what makes life work on a lot of different levels. ~~ I do this about the world and kind of assume everyone is doing it to some degree~~ anyway on this level, I was able to experientially see how humans synchronize themselves into their sleep cycles. I trust it’s able to be extrapolated to lots of elements of life as well.

It’s rare that I fall asleep for the night with the boys, I’ve got my me-time to get done a lot of the creative projects and the dishes etc. I usually get to bed long after everyone’s asleep. When I do get into bed, I’m treated to three people in synchronized sleeping. I squeeze in next to sOMa, who may or may not stir, setting off a chain reaction that gets everyone into the dance. Even their breathing gets into a rhythm. I imagine that heartbeats are too.

A similar phenomena is evident when I’m putting the boys to sleep. I lay with them until they’ve fallen asleep and then slip out. The moment that sleep happens is a precious one and witnessing it (or maybe even enabling it…see below) with the boys is one of the most spiritual, magical, mystic, not to mention Intimate experiences of my life. It’s especially profound if they hit the wall together. Happens maybe one out of seven sleeps.

I noticed early on with sOMa that there was some distinctive twitching that happens the moment he falls asleep. Through careful observation over a few years, I developed a sense around it whereby I would feel it come on. Almost like a wave.

Then after OshA came along, I noticed not only that the same phenomena occurred but that I was able to be more a part of it somehow. It’s hard to explain but when I feel him start to slide that way, like he’s at the point of deciding to be OK with letting go, I also start to slide away. I go into a kind of trance but I feel an energy coursing through me. Very prominently felt in my elbows, strangely enough. The elbows, I gather are like the hugging levers and we all know that hugs are wonderful expressions of intimacy. Perhaps this is a clue.

Anyway as I’m trancing out on the sliding sensation and my elbows are “carrying the charge” a visualization comes into my mind. It’s pretty much the same every time of a pulsing circle suspended in a blackness. It has whisps of bright that seem to snap or explode off of it while the circle is spinning with random erraticness but also some what constantly within that. And it’s like when the bright whisps snap and crack, its with a rhythm that indicates a progression into his sleep.

It is so neat and I’m often startled (I’ve occasionally awakened OshA) by the drop. It feels like my mind is holding the space for his process and then when he goes into the other side it leaves me hanging, like ghasping. But oh so satisfied too.

And like I said, when they hit it together it’s an incredible ride. Surfing the ethereal.

But on top of this there is also the intimacy of cuddles and connection. The mornings when Laura gets out of bed to go work and OshA stirs, I spring from snuggles with his older brother and cuddle him back into the safety of connection. Our physical bodies are so important on some levels.

And safety is a really important element of it. One of the most horrific things I was told as a parent to be was:

“You’re gonna have times where they’re just not co-operating and that’s when you have to let them cry it out. Put them in their crib, close the door and let them cry it out. It’s not gonna be easy the first time but you gotta break-em and then it will be all right…”

BREAK THEM?!?!?

I still can’t believe that this is commonly disseminated advice. How is a child supposed to feel safe when their screams of anguish are being ignored.

Sleeping with our children is a really effective way to help them feel valued, comfortable and most of all safe. It satisfies many important biological needs, including the most important need for physical contact.

I feel so blessed to be sharing in this gift of connection with my family. sOMa’s getting older now and I’m curious to see how the family bed will transform over the next few years. I for one hope that we can keep it going forever. I’m not sure what I’ll do without it. Through our family bed experience, I’ve found a bliss that I’d searched for my whole life. I trust that by sharing a bed with their family for as long as they need, that my children are not going to have the same search.

Just a hunch. Now it’s time to go to bed.

posts.title: sOMa (While You Were Sleeping)
posts.content:
sOMa (While You Were Sleeping)

Whispers of windy Sunshine
On Cedars,
Fresh as the River
Gurgles.

I can see the gurgles
As I hear them,

Like it seems I can hear
The shadows of the clouds
On Cedars;

Or perhaps it’s simply
Whispers of windy Sunshine…

posts.title: Heart Skipping
posts.content:
Heart Skipping

Heart skipping, alive with that song
The wind has been singing all night, all along.
A longing: whispers
As subtle as your breath.

And HOW I Love you!
Love you like a Lover Loves

And also as a dolphin Loves
The water, Loves
The moment where
The wind and water meet…

An explosive! fury of giggles, laughter:
Your misty words
And breath; the magic of silence
Between echoes

Of our heart singing, playfully along
To the Universal tune, this cosmic song:
An abundantly full Moon, patient and wise
Sighs…

And suddenly gives us a wink!

posts.title: Urban Wildcrafting Too
posts.content:
It’s amazing what happens when I’m connected to the Idea of being a Wildcrafter. It’s a different perspective. When I’m out and about, I’m continuously scouting and mentally noting where I saw that particularly juicy patch of Chickweed, having a look at how that Mullein grove was doing this year, seeing if there’s a way to get up to those Black Caps, pining for that new Nettle spot just around the corner. It’s a lifestyle that I’ve cultivated steadily over the last ten years and has been so fulfilling.

There has been this underlying thread of commerce underneath it all for me too. With every ‘product’ Idea I come up with, and there are lots, thought goes into a lot of different aspects of bringing that to market and thus, making a living at wildcrafting. I wouldn’t say quite that it has been elusive to this point; rather it has been a work in progress that appears to be culminating this summer.

I’ll be operating a table on Wednesday evening’s Bowen Rd. Farmer’s Market. I’ve toyed with the Idea for three years now and it’s finally coming together. I’m finding much pleasure and satisfaction in the process, of how this is steeping and becoming with every trip out to forage.

Which brings me to this: This Wildcrafting thing is by-and-large a study for me. My own homeschooling if you will. Self directed study of many aspects of plants and of our relationship to them. And so, within the context of it being a study, I’ve kind of had a bit of a plan for this season. that was this: 1. To get myself ready for and manifest a market table for the summer. 2. To cultivate a relationship with Hypericum Perforatum or more commonly, St. John’s Wort.

What I didn’t expect is that I would become so swept away along the way. In the planning dept., I kind of have the season mapped out. Right now is Nettle but then I’ve become swept up in the allure of Violets. Such delicate beauty and potent usefulness… not to mention flavor! Ok, so I took it in stride, I was suddenly “in to” violets. Still having some fun with them as the patch is persistent and also getting plenty of Nettle action…

And then Maple blossoms hit. OK this is exciting! Who’d a thunk that tree flowers were such an abundant source of food?! Maples put out Big Beefy Blossoms and lots and lots of them! I don’t even have the nutrition deets on them yet (anybody know?) but I do know that it’s a source of sweet and green. So big and full and LOTS!

So far we’ve used them as fritters and as a great addition to Crepe batters. I’ve gone out on two harvesting journeys at the local patches (which are all over. Maples are pretty ubiquitous). and have come out abundantly packed. The drying racks have been full for a few days. I’m keen to get some more too.

This urban wild is sustaining me on a lot of levels. On a very deep level, this continuing study is helping to mitigate some of the effects of urban noise pollution and reminding me of how Mighty nature truly is.

 

More Urban Wildcrafting shots can be found here.

posts.title: Congregation
posts.content:
Congregation

It’s in the laughter somewhere
Where
The space is superconnected;
That self-concocted soul-serum.
And when we find it when it’s there….
Then there is a
Magic
Infused Laughter somewhere

That seeks only to self-replicate.
A virus spread by smiles
That congregate in everywhere

Like

Sunspots on Droplets
On Vanilla Leaf that day…

Brought a laughter; a smile inside…
Magic.

posts.title: Finding Phalaris….
posts.content:
It’s finally time to meet a new ally. I’ve known for what seems now like forever, that this common grass, phalaris arundinacea, is a source of exogenous N,N-Dimethyltryptamine (DMT). For those who’ve been following this blog, you’ll know that DMT is an important component of Ayahuasca, that potent Amazonian Sacramental brew. It’s also an important chemical for our processes as humans as we make it regularly. It has been found in many tissues in the body and they hypothesize that we create this neurotransmitter in the pineal gland for a few different reasons, one of which is to provide the special effects for our dreamscapes.

It’s apparently also where we all go when we are born… and when we die. Apparently our brains are flooded with this magical chemical at those seminal watershed times in our lives.

My experience with taking DMT has been limited to its presence in the Aya brew that is Daime. I’ve been very curious about the experience of tripping on smoked DMT however. The reports I’ve read touch at what my experience with the first Daime ceremony was like, only more fantastical, surreal (or omni-real) and potentially quite scary as instant ego-loss is wont to be. Apparently it’s not uncommon for people to meet little alien-creatures when playing with pure DMT. As DMT is not so much a recreational thing, it’s in pretty low demand on the street and so pretty hard to find. So I’ll just have to make it myself. Thus Phalaris.

There are hundreds of plants out there that contain DMT. Popular sources are the Acacia family of plants from Down Under. I was once told by Horatio that the Daime brew contained “…about as much DMT as a Tomato”. I think that it may exist in trace amounts in a lot of so-far undocumented places. It’s also all around us here in Canada in one of the most common grasses out there. Reed Canary grass. Phalaris Arundinacae. Try saying that three times fast.

I knew also that this was a common grass, and that it got pretty big. I’d been non-chalantley trying to identify it for years but haven’t had a precise ID. Any google images were always confusing as there are what looks to be many different grasses by the same name. What I found out on my most recent quest is that the plant’s inflorescence changes dramatically through its life cycle. It’s strange though, now that I’ve got a positive ID, It’s everywhere.

The young leaves of Phalaris arundinacae contain anywhere from 0.007 to 0.121 % by weight. Sounds like not much. Fortunately DMT is one of the most potent neurochemicals out there, being active in very small doses. It still means that we need a LOT of plant matter to get any Spice. Fortunately as I said, it’s EVERYWHERE!

***
Part of me is after this as a study of what DMT is saying for my own experience as as pure a substance as I can get. I’m sure the DMT Flash will be an incredible experience! But I’m also very interested in creating a local “Ayahuasca” type elixir so that I can experience DMT as a gradual building story, creating the scene in my being. I have yet to discover the Mono-Amine-Oxidase-Inhibitor source locally though. I’ve heard that St. John’s Wort and Passionflower offer some potential in that realm but I wonder about any others… There is still much research to do.

The other part is to satisfy a visionary feeling that came over me some years ago about creating a sacramental snuff. I’m still waiting for further direction about that but have my suspicions that it will come to me as I begin the process of extraction.

It’s an exciting time for me as years of research is coming to fruition. It feels like it could be timely and maybe even a prophetic experience for me where I can claim my birthright….

Or something. First I gotta get the spice. There is much more grass to harvest.

info_pages.name: Your Comments
info_pages.body:
Dear Reader,

Initially, I set up this blog as a story of my journey. It wasn’t so much as an interactive place like blogs you may be used to. It’s not that I don’t appreciate comments, it’s more that I found that each individual piece was a chapter in a story and not a conversation, which is what I was seeing on blogs out there. Not only that, in playing around with the ‘theme editor’, I deleted the comment space and didn’t save the code

If you would like to comment on anything you read here, or have anything you’d like to express to me in general, you can email me at:

fungus.is@gmail.com

Someday, if I figure out how, there just may be a section of the blog created for articulate, intelligent and informative comments from readers. So when you write, please specify any aversion to being published. Also, If I ever figure out how to write what was deleted, I just may put it back in… for kicks.

Thank you

MyKL
